title: Macroprudential Regulation, Quantitative Easing, and Bank Lending
abstract: We show that widely used macroprudential regulations that rely on historical cost accounting (HCA) to insulate banks’ balance sheets from financial market volatility significantly affect the transmission of monetary policy onto bank lending. Using detailed supervisory data from Italian banks, we find that HCA mutes the transmission of quantitative easing and other monetary policies that affect the long end of the yield curve, weakening the effectiveness of interventions aimed at reducing firm credit constraints. We suggest alternative policies that have the benefits of HCA but allow monetary policy to pass through.

author.Unknown*: Wisconsin School of Business, University of Wisconsin-Madison,United States

article.1. Related Literature:
Issue Section: Article Editor: Itay Goldstein Itay Goldstein Editor Search for other works by this author on: Oxford Academic Google Scholar

Sovereign securities are a major part of banks’ asset holdings, and volatility in sovereign asset prices can lead to financial instability and credit crunches. Recent examples of this phenomenon include the sovereign-bank “diabolic loop” in Europe in the early 2010s, the collapse of Silicon Valley Bank (SVB) and First Republic Bank, and the distress at some regional U.S. banks in 2023.1To help contain the risks from this volatility, regulators use accounting-based macroprudential policies, a crucial part of the broader macro-stabilization tool kit.2During the European sovereign-bank crisis of the early 2010s, most sovereign assets held by European banks were valued using historical cost accounting (HCA) to shield banks’ regulatory capital and lending capacity from sovereign bond volatility. When the crisis ebbed in 2018, regulators used mark-to-market accounting (MMA) more widely, allowing sovereign price changes to pass through onto banks’ balance sheets (Figure 1).3Alongside these accounting-based macroprudential policies, central banks also use yield curve management policies, such as forward guidance and quantitative easing (QE), to stabilize economies. However, little is known about how these macroprudential tools mediate the transmission of monetary policy onto the availability of bank credit to firms. Our paper addresses this gap.

Figure 1: Macroprudential accounting regime of sovereign securities and QE announcements The figure shows the evolution of some key aspects of the European Union macroprudential accounting regime that banks use to value their holdings of sovereign securities when computing regulatory net worth (see Section 2 for more details) and the two main announcements of the PSPP, which is the ECB’s largest QE program. Our main focus is on the European Central Bank’s largest QE program—the Public Sector Purchase Programme (PSPP)—together with the changes in macroprudential regulation shown inFigure 1. In particular, we study the introduction of the PSPP in January 2015, when HCA was used to value most sovereign securities, and the relaunch of the PSPP in 2019, when MMA was more prevalent. These policy changes, along with credit register and granular supervisory data, allow us to make progress on causal inference and identify the pathways through which macroprudential tools shape the transmission of QE onto bank lending. We then go beyond this specific setting and broaden the analysis to understand the interaction between macroprudential accounting tools and more general monetary policies that affect the yield curve, such as forward guidance and other asset purchase programs. Throughout, we focus on banks and firms in Italy, the largest country affected by the sovereign-bank “diabolic loop.”

The impact of macroprudential rules, such as HCA, on the transmission of monetary policy interventions like QE is an empirical question and depends on the mechanism through which QE works. Notably, if the bank recapitalization or portfolio rebalancing channels are salient, HCA might inhibit the transmission of QE onto bank lending. According to the recapitalization channel, an increase in asset prices triggered by QE has a positive impact on banks’ net worth and lending capacity. In the case of the portfolio rebalancing channel, banks sell their appreciated assets in order to rebalance their portfolio toward riskier, higher-return assets, such as loans. For either channel, the widespread use of HCA would limit the pass-through of any QE-related asset price increase onto the balance sheet of banks, attenuating the impact on bank lending. But the macroprudential accounting regime could be irrelevant for the transmission of QE and other monetary policies if these interventions operate in other ways, such as by easing liquidity conditions for banks or increasing the market price of bank equity.

There are significant challenges in identifying whether the macroprudential accounting regime mediates the transmission of QE onto bank loan supply. First, both macroprudential and monetary policy respond endogenously to current and expected economic conditions (Nakamura and Steinsson 2018).4Second, QE is often implemented alongside other policies that can also affect bank lending. And third, accounting-based macroprudential policies are highly targeted tools, and changes in the price of an asset can affect a bank’s net worth very differently depending on where the asset is recorded on the balance sheet.5We address these issues by using monthly supervisory Italian bank balance sheet data and monthly firm-bank-level data from the Italian credit register. The relatively high frequency of monthly data reduces the risk of contamination from other policies. The use of credit register data allows us to nonparametrically control for loan demand (Khwaja and Mian 2008). And granular supervisory data on banks’ asset holdings, including the specific location on banks’ balance sheets and their accounting treatment, allow us to precisely measure the pass-through of QE on each bank.

We find that HCA mutes the impact of QE on bank lending. In both 2015 and 2019, only banks exposed to the PSPP through their holdings of MMA sovereign assets increased lending in response to the PSPP. The economic impact of the PSPP is vastly different across the two periods, consistent with the variation in the use of HCA. In 2015, HCA was widely used to protect the banking system, and for sovereign securities, MMA covered only those classified in the trading book. Because few banks had significant trading book holdings, the 2015 PSPP increased lending by approximately €214 million according to a back-of-the-envelope calculation. The lending response in 2019 is about 56 times larger, at about €12 billion, reflecting the fact that MMA had become more prevalent, increasing the size of banks’ exposure to PSPP-eligible asset purchases. We also find that much of this additional lending went to less risky firms, and banks generally tried to reduce their risk exposure after the PSPP with riskier banks derisking the most.

A battery of robustness tests rules out concerns related to anticipatory biases, selection into exposure, and seasonality, and show that our results are robust when we control for several banks’ characteristics as well as banks’ exposure to other central bank interventions. We also show that interest rates declined by about 100 and 60 basis points (bps) for loans originated by exposed banks in 2015 and 2019, respectively, relative to less exposed ones. The decrease in rates, along with the increase in loan volume, implies that we are identifying changes in supply as opposed to movements in credit demand.6

We then turn our attention to identifying the mechanism behind the transmission of QE to discern the role played by macroprudential accounting rules. In both 2015 and 2019, the recapitalization channel appears to be the key mechanism through which QE affects bank lending. That is, capital gains on MMA assets eligible for purchase under the PSPP are significantly associated with increased lending, and undercapitalized banks that held large amounts of such assets increased lending the most. We also test for the salience of the portfolio rebalancing channel and find there is a significant impulse in trading activity after the PSPP announcement among banks with a larger MMA PSPP exposure (ie, more exposed banks sell more eligible securities), but only in 2019. Even after controlling for this trading activity, we find that lending increased the most among banks that experienced the largest capital gains in their MMA portfolio, and the transmission of QE is essentially entirely driven by the recapitalization channel.

Do these results generalize beyond QE? Both QE and other monetary policies, such as forward guidance, influence long-term yields and could affect banks’ lending behavior similarly. We thus extend our analysis to study how monetary policy shocks to the long end of the yield curve immediately after the European Central Bank’s policy announcements—so-called “pathrate shocks”—affect bank loan supply between 2010 and 2020. Similar to QE, these more general shocks affect loan supply only through banks’ holdings of MMA securities, and the effects are larger for less capitalized banks. For a bank at the median of the MMA exposure, a 1-percentage-point negative shock is associated with a 0.41-percentage-point increase in lending if the bank is at the 25th percentile of the distribution of Tier 1 capital to risk-weighted assets, and 0.29 percentage points if the bank is at the 75th percentile.

These results imply that while HCA can shield bank equity when sovereign assets lose value, HCA also prevents central banks’ yield curve management policies from increasing loan supply to firms during periods of distress. We use a simple analytic framework to elaborate this basic intuition and show that a hybrid accounting approach that passes through changes in the risk-free rate, but fixes the sovereign spread at its historical value, can both stabilize banks’ equity and allow monetary policy to stimulate loan supply to firms. More generally, our results suggest that HCA can interact with other policies that work through banks’ regulatory capital. For instance, we point out that HCA and a static capital requirement—a combination frequently used in practice—is equivalent to MMA combined with a tightening of the capital requirement in response to a reduction in yields.

These results have important implications. The debate over MMA versus HCA in bank regulation has generally focused on the trade-off between the benefits of providing timely information to ensure market discipline versus the costs of excess volatility and its impact on banks’ investment decisions and asset prices.7Very little attention has been devoted to understanding whether these macroprudential accounting policies influence other macro-stabilization tools, such as central bank yield curve management. To our knowledge, this is the first paper to suggest that attempts to stabilize the banking system through HCA might perversely make monetary policy less effective. Given the increased use of central bank yield curve management policies, the frictions imposed by HCA on bank lending can be sizeable.

A related contribution centers on the efficacy of monetary policy at the zero lower bound. There is considerable debate about the effectiveness of monetary policy when interest rates are close to zero or negative, with many macroeconomic models noting that monetary policy may no longer be effective at the zero lower bound (see the discussion inMcKay, Nakamura, and Steinsson 2016). However, empirical studies, such asSwanson and Williams (2014), suggest that longer-term yields remain sensitive to new information, such as monetary policy announcements, even when the policy rate is at the zero lower bound. Our findings extend those inSwanson and Williams (2014), showing that unconventional monetary policy can directly relieve financing constraints at firms, but only through the effects transmitted by banks’ MMA assets.8
A small but growing literature analyzes the effects of macroprudential accounting rules on banks’ investment and risk-taking decisions.Begenau et al. (2023)show that accounting rules that allow for delayed loss recognition are crucial to explain banks’ response to negative shocks.Fuster and Vickery (2018)andKim, Kim, and Ryan (2019)find mixed results on the increase in prevalence of MMA on risk-taking decisions, but they document that banks reclassify securities toward sections of their balance sheets that are subject to HCA.Granja (2023)shows that weaker U.S. banks reclassified securities in 2022 to take advantage of HCA. While most of this literature focuses on the effects of macroprudential accounting rules on banks’ decisions, our paper is the first—to our knowledge—that studies how these rules shape the effects of other major interventions such as monetary policy and capital requirements.

Our work is related to the literature that studies the interaction between macroprudential and monetary policies. Relative to this literature, our innovation is the focus on accounting-based macroprudential rules, which are widely used in practice. The theoretical arguments inDi Tella (2019)andFarhi and Werning (2016)suggest that macroprudential policies that stabilize banks’ net worth can reduce booms and busts, and the joint implementation of monetary and macroprudential policies can further dampen the real effects of adverse shocks (see, for instance,Van der Ghote 2021).Aiyar, Calomiris, and Wieladek (2016)find little evidence of an interaction between conventional monetary policy and capital requirements, andAltavilla, Laeven, and Peydró (2020)andBruno, Shim, and Shin (2017)study whether accommodative macroprudential environments tend to boost the effects of monetary policy easing. However, they use broad indexes of macroprudential regulation rather than focusing on the specific channels of the accounting-based macroprudential policies as we do.9

We also build on the growing literature on QE. In the case of the PSPP, several studies have focused on the asset price responses to the program’s announcement. Notably, the evidence inAndrade et al. (2016)andAltavilla, Carboni, and Motto (2021)suggests that the PSPP’s announcement led to a drop in European sovereign yields and an increase in asset prices, including the stock prices of banks. Extrapolating from this asset price response, the model-based results inAndrade et al. (2016)suggest that the PSPP led to an increase in output in part through the bank lending channel. A few papers study important aspects of the PSPP using bank-firm-level data. For instance,Bittner et al. (2023)analyze the interaction with negative interest rates, andNogueira, Farinha, and Blattner (2023)compare the effects of the PSPP with those of other monetary policies. We focus instead on the interaction with macroprudential accounting regulations. More generally, the transmission of QE onto bank loan supply and the overall efficacy of QE remain open questions; see the survey inFabo et al. (2021). Our results on the effects of the macroprudential accounting regimes may suggest why the effects of QE can be heterogeneous and difficult to detect.

Beyond micro and macroprudential channels, the evidence inPeydró, Polo, and Sette (2021)observes that the transmission of monetary policy onto bank lending can be impaired if banks hoard liquidity or engage in securities trading instead of lending (see alsoAbbassi et al. [2016]in the case of German banks). Using Italian micro data similar to ours,Peydró, Polo, and Sette (2021)observe that banks more exposed to the PSPP may have engaged in more securities trading. However, those authors do not directly measure the effects of the PSPP onto loan supply, nor do they study the role of macroprudential accounting regulations.

Our analysis of the bank lending channel of QE is also closely related to work based on U.S. data (Foley-Fisher, Ramcharan, and Yu 2016;Rodnyansky and Darmouni 2017;Chakraborty, Goldstein, and MacKinlay 2020;Luck and Zimmermann 2020).10With detailed supervisory data on banks’ asset holdings, we can make progress in understanding the effects of heterogeneity in the application of MMA and HCA in asset valuation. The relatively high frequency of our monthly data also helps us to be clear on the counterfactual and exclude alternative interpretations stemming from other economic news. Moreover, because we observe the near-universe of Italian firms, our results are less likely to be sensitive to the choice of firms in any given sample. More generally, our approach builds on the broader literature examining the bank lending channel using micro data, such asJiménez et al. (2014), and classic studies such asKashyap and Stein (2000)andPeek and Rosengren (2000).

Finally, because of the link that we establish between the use of HCA to value some assets and capital requirements, our work is also related to some of the existing theoretical and quantitative analysis that links financial regulation with banks’ assets holdings.Van den Heuvel (2008)formalizes the idea that higher capital requirements reduce the riskiness of banks’ investments, andPancost and Robatto (2023)quantify this effect to be of first-order importance.Bahaj and Malherbe (2020)show that higher capital requirements can increase bank lending by making banks safer and increasing the loan repayments that benefit shareholders. Our mechanism is complementary in that HCA combined with a static capital requirement is equivalent to MMA and a type of time-varying capital requirement. In this sense, our work is related to the papers that study time-varying capital requirements (Davydiuk 2017;Malherbe 2020;Canzoneri et al. 2023).
article.2. Background: The Monetary Policy Trans:
The broader literature has identified several channels through which various types of QE programs can affect asset prices and bank lending (Brunnermeier and Sannikov 2013;Krishnamurthy and Vissing-Jorgensen 2011), and this section reviews the main theories. We also summarize basic (macroprudential) accounting rules, which banks are subject to when valuing their security holdings.

A key transmission mechanism is the so-called “recapitalization channel.” According to this channel, an increase in security prices triggered by QE would raise the value of banks’ securities holdings and banks’ net worth. This would allow leveraged-constrained banks that are exposed to the PSPP to expand their loan supply with a stronger effect for undercapitalized banks. This channel is likely to be important in our empirical setting, as the PSPP increased the market value of PSPP-eligible securities (Andrade et al. 2016;Altavilla, Carboni, and Motto 2021) and Italian banks were undercapitalized in our sample period, especially circa 2015. In our setting, the recapitalization channel predicts that the PSPP’s impact on lending should vary with the ex ante cross-sectional differences in banks’ holdings of PSPP-eligible securities, as well as with cross-sectional differences in banks’ capitalization.

The liquidity channel is another potentially important channel through which the PSPP might affect banks’ loan supply. This channel builds on the idea that central bank asset purchases directly increase the liquidity of eligible assets, allowing exposed banks to more easily liquidate such assets to increase lending without generating a price impact. The liquidity channel predicts, in our setting, that the PSPP’s impact should vary with the ex ante cross-sectional differences in banks’ liquidity and their PSPP exposure via their holdings of eligible securities.

Another important mechanism is the so-called “portfolio rebalancing channel.” According to this channel, banks may sell the appreciated PSPP-eligible securities and rebalance their asset holdings toward investments with a higher expected return, such as loans. In the portfolio rebalancing case, the PSPP’s loan supply impact should vary with the cross-sectional differences in ex post asset sales at exposed banks.11

Some channels, such as the recapitalization and portfolio rebalancing channel, might be crucially affected by the pass-through of market prices and yields onto banks’regulatorycapital. This is the case, for instance, if banks’ decisions are affected by regulatory constraints rather than market-based leverage constraints. Because regulatory capital is essentially given by the value of banks’ assets net of their liabilities, the prevailing accounting rules used to value banks’ assets, including security holdings, can be important for the transmission of monetary policy. We now briefly describe these rules for Italian banks.

A security can be valued using historical cost accounting (HCA) or mark-to-market accounting (MMA) for the purpose of capital requirement regulation. Under HCA, a security’s value depends on its purchase price, and changes in market prices do not affect its regulatory net worth. In contrast, under MMA, the security is valued using current market prices, and changes in market conditions pass through onto the bank’s regulatory net worth.

Whether a security is valued using MMA or HCA depends on how a bank classifies the security on its balance sheet: holdings of the same securities can be valued differently depending on their classification. In general, banks can classify a security in one of three ways:held to maturity, available for sale, or atfair value through profit or loss. The latter is often referred to as thetrading book.12If the bank plans to hold the security until maturity, it typically classifies the security as held to maturity. If the bank intends to keep the security on its balance sheet but retain the option to sell, the bank classifies the security as available for sale (AFS). Securities that are traded more frequently are classified in the trading book. Key for our analysis is that regulators heavily scrutinize these classifications, so that the classifications are sticky and accurate, as banks cannot easily reclassify securities without prior regulatory approval.13In our empirical setting, we show that holdings of PSPP-eligible securities are indeed very sticky over time, and we conduct tests to rule out the possibility that banks select into exposure in anticipation of the PSPP announcement (seeSection 4.3.2).

Figure 2summarizes the macroprudential accounting rules for Italian banks. The rules governing sovereign held-to-maturity and trading book securities have been relatively unchanged. Held-to-maturity securities are valued using HCA, and trading book securities are valued using MMA. But for AFS securities issued by euro area central governments—which are eligible for purchase under the PSPP—regulators have applied different regimes over time. To insulate the banking system from the effects of the sovereign crisis, during the early 2010s and until the end of 2017, regulators permitted banks to use HCA to value AFS sovereign securities.14But as of 2018, regulators required AFS securities to be valued using MMA.15

Figure 2: Sovereign security classification and prevalent macroprudential accounting regime This figure shows how identical sovereign assets on the balance sheet are valued differently depending on their regulatory classification, for the purpose of computing regulatory capital. Between 2010 and 2018, sovereign assets classified as available for sale were valued at historical cost. Between 2018 and 2019, sovereign assets classified as available for sale were then marked to market. In 2020, amid the COVID-19 pandemic, regulators introduced a prudential filter that allowed banks to again value available-for-sale sovereign assets at historical cost, based on the market value as of December 31, 2019. Assets classified as held to maturity are always valued at historical cost, whereas those in the trading book are always marked to market. Open in new tab Download slide

This regulatory structure creates variation in the accounting rules used to classify sovereign securities, both at any given time within the cross-section of securities and over time for the AFS classification. Our analysis exploits these sources of variation to calculate a bank’s exposure to the PSPP and other central bank shocks to the yield curve in order to understand how these policies affect bank credit supply. The first PSPP announcement took place in January 2015, when only trading book sovereign securities were valued at MMA. But the 2019 resumption of the PSPP occurred when both AFS and trading book sovereign securities were valued at MMA. Thus, in addition to the fact that banks had different holdings of HCA and MMA securities at the time of each announcement, we also exploit the change in the classification of AFS sovereign assets from HCA to MMA between 2015 and 2019 to identify the role of macroprudential accounting rules in mediating the effects of the PSPP.
article.3. Data:
On January 22, 2015, the European Central Bank (ECB) announced the PSPP program—to be started in March—consisting of the purchases of about €50 billion per month of euro-denominated debt securities issued by euro area central governments and supranational institutions, with residual maturity between 2 and 30 years.16The program was paused at the end of December 2018, and the ECB announced on September 12, 2019, a restart as of November of that year.17This subsection describes the data we use to measure banks’ exposure to the PSPP and identify the transmission mechanism. We draw on several sources, primarily collected by the Bank of Italy.

First, we use the Italian Central Credit Register, which contains information for each firm-bank lending relationship. The credit register is a monthly panel data set at the firm-bank-time level. That is, each entry denotes the amount of lending made by a particular bank to a particular nonfinancial firm in a given month. All loans above €30,000 are included in the register, thereby making the coverage near-universal. Second, we use AnaCredit and Taxia, two data sets with information collected by the Bank of Italy, to retrieve interest rate data on new term loans. AnaCredit has data at a monthly frequency but only covers 2019, whereas Taxia has data for 2015 but only at a quarterly frequency. Third, we use the Bank of Italy Credit and Financial Institutions’ Supervisory Reports to obtain banks’ balance sheet data. A key element of these data is represented by banks’ security holdings at the ISIN level, and for each ISIN, the breakdown of the holdings classified as held to maturity, available for sale, or in the trading book. This level of granularity allows us to construct each bank’s measure of exposure to the 2019 and 2015 QE announcements and, together with security prices from the Centralised Securities Database of the Eurosystem, banks’ trading activity.

In most of our analyses, we work with monthly data over a 12-month window around the QE announcement dates (ie, around September 12, 2019, andJanuary 22, 2015). We focus on banking groups (hereinafter referred to as “banks”) for which the bank holding company is a joint stock company. That is, we exclude mutual and cooperative banks because they are subject to different regulations. We also drop foreign banks, leaving our final sample at 90 banks for 2019 and 95 banks for 2015. Our focus on banking groups (as opposed to single banks) is motivated by the fact that regulators set key regulations, such as capital requirements, at the group level.18Our approach is similar to that used in recent papers with Italian banking data, such asBenetton and Fantino (2021)andBottero et al. (2022).

Table 1provides key summary statistics about the firm-bank lending relationships and the interest rate data. Our main analysis uses the monthly growth rate of the amount borrowed for each firm-bank pair with an ongoing lending relationship. Focusing on firms with at least two lending relationships (Khwaja and Mian 2008), we have access to more than 8 million observations for the 2019 episode and about 6 million for 2015.

Table 2contains summary statistics about the banks in our sample. Holdings of PSPP-eligible securities were substantial. Banks held on average 17.06% and 11.44% of assets in securities eligible to be purchased under the PSPP prior to the 2019 and 2015 announcements, respectively. Holdings of eligible securities valued at historical cost were 11.12% and 11.00% in 2019 and 2015, respectively, and holdings of eligible MMA securities were 6.00% and 0.45% in 2019 and 2015, respectively. The 2015 mark-to-market figure appears small, as it includes only securities in the trading book as discussed inSection 2, but there is significant heterogeneity across banks that gives rise to a large cross-sectional variation: the coefficient of variation is in fact higher in 2015 than in 2019.

article.4. Macroprudential Regime and the Impact:
This section shows that the macroprudential accounting regime mediates the impact of the PSPP on bank lending. We begin with the 2019 PSPP announcement, when mark-to-market accounting (MMA) applied to PSPP-eligible securities in the available-for-sale (AFS) and trading book portfolios. We then move to the 2015 PSPP announcement, when regulators valued sovereign AFS securities using historical cost accounting (HCA) and, among PSPP-eligible, only those in the trading book were valued at MMA. The section concludes with a formal test of the importance of MMA in the transmission mechanism across the two time periods and a battery of robustness tests.

4.1. The effects of the PSPP: The basic results

This section presents the basic relationship between the PSPP and bank lending, beginning with the 2019 announcement and then repeating the analysis for the 2015 announcement. Informed by theories of the monetary transmission mechanism, the research design combines the cross-sectional variation in banks’ exposure to the PSPP via their holdings of MMA-eligible securities with monthly data on lending. The research design then uses a difference-in-difference framework to exploit the discrete nature of the ECB’s PSPP announcements. This difference-in-difference framework employs a narrow time window around each PSPP announcement with monthly data to isolate the effects of the PSPP from other ongoing ECB policies, such as forward guidance, negative interest rates, and the targeted longer-term refinancing operations.19For each announcement, the baseline estimation window spans both the 6 months before and 6 months after the announcement.

The design also uses firm-by-year-month fixed effects to absorb nonparametrically loan demand at the firm-by-year-month to identify the PSPP-induced bank lending supply response (Khwaja and Mian 2008). Additionally, because the Italian credit register contains the near-universe of the bank-firm credit relationships, we can measure the effects of QE on business lending more completely than inferring a treatment effect based on a selected sample of larger firms, usually from DealScan or through regulatory data in the United States.

The dependent variable is the monthly growth rate in lending at the intensive margin, namely, the change in the logarithm of disbursed loans from bank b to firm f between month t and t –1, log( ⁠ L b , f , t ) – log( ⁠ L b , f , t − 1 ) ⁠ . The baseline specification for the 2019 announcement is Δ log ⁡ L b , f , t = ∑ τ ≠ 2019 m 8 β τ × I τ × Q E b + ∑ τ ≠ 2019 m 8 γ τ × I τ × Y b + δ Z b , t + ψ b + ψ f , t + ε b , f , t . (1)

The variableQEbis a measure of a bank’s exposure to the PSPP based on the bank’s holdings of securities eligible for purchase under the PSPP. We computeQEbin the month before the announcement, so in the case of the September 2019 announcement, we use banks’ asset holdings at the end of August 2019. We then interactQEbwith a set of time dummiesIτ⁠, one for each month, dropping the one that corresponds to the preannouncement month. Thus, our coefficients of interest are theβτ⁠. This sequence captures the effects of the PSPP announcement on bank lending supply and allows us to check for any possible pre-trend in lending growth. The termsψbandψf,tare bank and firm-by-time fixed effects, respectively, and standard errors are clustered at the bank level.

To exclude alternative interpretations, the baseline specification includes three controls, captured by the vectorYband the termZb,tinEquation (1). First, we include bank size using the logarithm of total assets as of the month before the announcement, interacted with time dummies centered on the announcement month. Second, because banks’ holdings of central bank reserves as well as borrowing from the ECB have been shown to independently affect bank behavior (Diamond, Jiang, and Ma 2024;Drechsler et al. 2016), we include central bank reserves (defined as cash plus deposits at the central bank as a fraction of total assets, computed before the announcement and interacted with time dummies centered on the announcement month) and the logarithmic change in the euro value that bankbborrows from the ECB at timet, denoted byZb,t⁠. Additional controls, including those related to bank characteristics and banks’ exposure to other ECB policies, are added in the robustness checks ofSection 4.3.

We first follow the literature and defineQEbusing the broadest exposure definition: the ratio of a bank’s securities that are eligible to be purchased under the PSPP to total bank assets (Rodnyansky and Darmouni 2017;Luck and Zimmermann 2020). This broad exposure measure ignores the macroprudential-based accounting treatment of sovereign assets. Italian banks hold large amounts of sovereign securities, and in the month before the 2019 announcement, they had on average 17.1% of assets in PSPP-eligible securities.

Table 3presents the results. We begin by showing all the event-study coefficients inEquation (1)to be transparent, before moving to a more concise difference-in-difference specification for ease of exposition. From column 1 ofTable 3, there is no evidence that broad exposure to the PSPP is associated with an increase in lending after the announcement. The point estimates are small and show a pre-trend, and there is no clear difference between the pre- and post-announcement periods.20

Next, we redefine the measure of exposureQEbto consider the accounting-based macroprudential regulation in place in 2019. Specifically, we include only eligible securities that are valued at MMA. Based on the 2019 macroprudential rules, sovereign securities were valued at MMA if classified as either AFS or held in the trading book, which jointly account for 6.00% of total assets on average across banks.

The results of using only MMA eligible securities—shown in column 2—dramatically differ from those in column 1. Column 2 ofTable 3shows that banks exposed to the PSPP via their holdings of MMA securities increased lending almost immediately upon the PSPP’s announcement. This measure of exposure also shows no trend difference in lending growth between exposed and nonexposed banks in the period before the PSPP announcement (average March to July 2019=0.04⁠,p-value=.48⁠). The September 2019 coefficient is also significantly greater than the March-July 2019 average (difference: 0.18,p-value<.01⁠). Column 2 also shows a positive effect in December 2019 (ie, 1 month after the actual restart of the purchases) and no subsequent reversal in loan growth.21

A back-of-the-envelope calculation using the estimates in column 2 suggests that the PSPP increased loan supply by about €12 billion in this sample period. We obtain this number based on the counterfactual in which all the banks have zero exposure, and conservatively use the lending responses in September and December 2019, that is, the only 2 months with a statistically significant coefficient. The effect is slightly higher at €13 billion if we instead use the average lending response between September and December 2019 (average September to December 2019=0.156⁠,p-value=.048⁠).22A similar calculation implies that if all banks’ holdings of eligible securities had been valued according to MMA, the overall effect on lending would have been almost three times bigger at €32.6 billion.

Column 3 addresses possible measurement errors in the PSPP exposure. It uses a dummy variable, comparing banks in the top tercile of the exposure distribution with those in the bottom tercile, as in, for example,Chakraborty, Goldstein, and MacKinlay (2020). The results are unchanged. The most exposed banks—those in the top tercile—increased loan supply by 2.61 percentage points right after the 2019 PSPP announcement and by 3.37 percentage points in December 2019, relative to less exposed banks. We again find no significant difference in loan growth in the period before the announcement: the March to August 2019 average is 0.57 (p-value=.42⁠).

As a further check, column 4 uses a more concise difference-in-difference approach. This is a simplepostindicator variable interacted with the MMA exposure to compute the average impact of the 2019 PSPP on bank lending over the 6 months after the announcement (ie, September 2019 to February 2020). Column 4 shows that the interaction term is significant, suggesting that while the lending response was immediate, it also appears persistent over the 6 months. Panel A ofFigure 3provides a simple visualization of the results of the difference-in-difference approach that replicates columns 1–3 ofTable 3. The results are essentially unchanged.

The remaining columns ofTable 3repeat these exercises for the 2015 PSPP announcement, using a sample centered around the January 2015 announcement. Unlike 2019, in 2015 regulators used MMA only for trading book assets; sovereign assets classified as AFS—most sovereign assets—were valued at HCA in 2015. This difference means that the set of banks that were highly exposed to the 2019 PSPP via their MMA holdings of eligible securities differ from the highly exposed set in 2015. This variation helps address identification concerns based on unobserved bank business model characteristics that might correlate with both the MMA exposure and the lending of a bank, helping us triangulate the role of the macroprudential regime itself in transmitting yield curve shocks onto bank lending.

Focusing on the 2015 PSPP announcement, column 5 ofTable 3replicates the specification in column 1. As in 2019, broad exposure to the PSPP is not associated with an increase in loan supply after the 2015 announcement.23Column 6 ofTable 3uses banks’ exposure to the PSPP based only on MMA eligible securities according to the 2015 macroprudential accounting framework (ie, those classified in the trading book). Banks more exposed to the PSPP via MMA securities increased lending immediately upon the PSPP announcement. There is again no pre-trend (average July to November 2014: 0.13,p-value=.50⁠), and the January 2015 coefficient is significantly greater than the July to November 2014 average (difference: 0.30,p-value<.01⁠).24

Column 7 ofTable 3uses an exposure dummy to deal with possible measurement error. In 2015, holdings of MMA eligible securities were limited to the trading book, and thus, much smaller than in 2019 and with a highly skewed distribution. Hence, we define the 2015 exposure dummy as equal to one if a bank is in the top 15% of the distribution; this corresponds approximately to the median of the distribution of MMA eligible securities relative to total assets, conditional on strictly positive holdings. The results remain robust to using the dichotomous measure of exposure. Loan growth increases by about 1.76 percentage points for highly exposed banks in January 2015, relative to less exposed ones.25

Finally, column 8 ofTable 3uses the more concise difference-in-difference specification, interacting the MMA exposure measure with apostindicator variable. The interaction is significant, suggesting that the lending impact lasted over the estimation window, even for 2015. And panel B ofFigure 3visualizes the results of replicating columns 5–7 with thepostindicator. The results are again essentially unchanged.

Despite the similarity of the 2015 results with those of the 2019 analysis, the implied effect of the 2015 PSPP announcement on loan supply is much smaller. The coefficients in column 6 ofTable 3imply an increase in lending of only €214 million in 2015, in contrast to the €12 billion in 2019 from column 2. The difference between 2019 and 2015 is large not just for the Italian banking system as a whole but also at every point in the distribution of exposure: compare panels A and B ofFigure 4, which plot the effect of the PSPP in the cross-section of banks with positive exposure. The very small 2015 lending effect reflects the narrow application of MMA in that period’s macroprudential accounting regime. That is, sovereign securities were valued at MMA in 2015 only if held in the trading book. In contrast, the 2019 macroprudential regulation required banks to use MMA for both AFS and trading book sovereign securities. Because banks record a significant amount of sovereign assets as AFS, the 2019 PSPP announcement induced a much bigger lending response relative to 2015.

In sum, the evidence suggests that the PSPP elicited a stronger lending response when a larger fraction of a bank’s eligible securities was valued at MMA. Using the fact that the only difference between the 2015 and 2019 regimes is the treatment of AFS sovereign assets, the next subsection nests the 2019 and 2015 specifications to formally test the importance of MMA in the transmission of the PSPP.

4.2. Combining the 2019 and 2015 samples: A formal test of the role of the macroprudential accounting regime

This subsection combines the 2015 and 2019 samples to formally test the role played by MMA in the transmission of the PSPP. This combination results in a single panel, where each subpanel spans a 12-month window centered on the 2019 and 2015 announcement months, respectively. For expositional clarity, we present here the results of a concise difference-in-difference (DiD) estimate and relegate the event study coefficients to Internet Appendix IX . Both approaches yield identical results. The DiD estimate uses an indicator variable post that equals one for the observations in the 6 months after each announcement, and another indicator variable I 2019 that equals one for the observations in the 12 months around the 2019 announcement. The estimating equation is: Δ log ⁡ L b , f , t = θ [ p o s t ] × Q E b A F S + λ [ p o s t ] × Q E b A F S × [ I 2019 ] + β Q E b T r a d i n g B o o k × [ p o s t ] + γ Y b + δ Z b , t + ψ b + ψ b × [ I 2019 ] + ψ f , t + ε b , f , t , where Q E b A F S denotes bank b ’s holding of available-for-sale securities, Q E b T r a d i n g B o o k denotes bank b ’s holding of trading book securities, and the other terms are the same as in Equation (1) , for we include the same controls as in the baseline. Note, however, that we allow the bank fixed effects to vary between the 2015 and 2019 windows (ie, we use bank-by-announcement fixed effects) to allow for the possibility that each bank’s trend in loan growth rates around the 2019 announcement differs from the 2015 one. Under the assumption that only MMA securities affect banks’ response to the PSPP, the coefficient λ —which captures the differential effect of the AFS exposure when AFS securities are valued at MMA—should be positive and statistically significant. In addition, the coefficient θ —which captures the baseline effect of the AFS exposure when AFS securities are valued at HCA—should not be significant. This test reflects the intuition that the only difference between 2015 and 2019 in the macroprudential accounting regime is the MMA treatment of AFS sovereign assets in 2019.

Table 4presents the results of this nested test and confirms the role played by MMA in transmitting the effects of QE. From column 1, the coefficientλofpost×QEbAFS×I2019is economically and statistically significant, implying that the impact of the 2019 PSPP on bank lending was larger among banks with greater AFS exposure, relative to the 2015 sample. A one-standard-deviation increase in AFS exposure in 2019 suggests a 0.9-percentage-point increase in lending in each of the post-announcement months. Also, the coefficientθon the base impact of AFS exposure (ie, the coefficient ofpost×QEbAFS)is not significant, which is consistent with the fact that, in 2015, eligible AFS assets were valued at HCA, limiting the pass-through of the PSPP onto banks’ balance sheets.

In addition, the point estimate in column 4 is much bigger than in column 3, consistent with the immediate announcement effects fromTable 3. Overall, these tests confirm the mediating role of macroprudential accounting regulation in the pass-through of quantitative easing onto bank lending.

4.3. Robustness analyses

We conduct a long list of robustness checks for both 2019 and 2015. Below is a summary of these tests, with the details in theInternet Appendix.

4.3.1. Effects on interest rates

If the PSPP caused an expansion in credit supply, the price of credit (ie, the interest rate on loans) should decline. In contrast, if our results reflect a coincidental increase in credit demand, interest rates should be nondecreasing in exposure to the PSPP. This section studies the impact of the PSPP on the interest rates of newly originated term loans with maturity greater than 1 year. These data are available only for a subset of banks—our final sample includes 43 banks in 2019 and 37 banks in 2015—and for firms with an overall outstanding loan balance at any given bank of at least €25,000 in 2019 and €75,000 in 2015.27The 2019 data are available at a monthly frequency, but the 2015 data are available only at a quarterly frequency.28Internet Appendix IVshows that the main results ofTable 3hold for the subsample of banks for which we have interest rate data.

We use a modified version of the baseline specification described inEquation (1)in which we continue to use firm-by-time fixed effects. The new dependent variable inTable 5is the interest rateib,f,ton a term loan originated at timetby bankband extended to firmf.29Because of the limited sample, we focus on the dummy measures of exposure to the PSPP to limit the effects of noise and outliers, as defined inSection 4.1. And for expositional clarity, we interact the exposure measure only with a dummy equal to one in the announcement period;Internet Appendix IVshows that the results are unchanged when we use the full set of event-study coefficients.Table 5shows that more exposed banks reduced the interest rate in comparison to less exposed ones after the announcement. The result is statistically significant, and the magnitude is economically important: more exposed banks reduce interest rates by about 60 bps in the 2019 announcement month (column 1 ofTable 5) and by 97 bps in the 2015 announcement quarter (column 2 ofTable 5). This suggests that both the 2019 and 2015 PSPP announcements generated a shift in the loan supply curve, consistent with an increase in quantities and a reduction in prices.

4.3.2. Other robustness checks

We run several robustness checks that support our main result. This section briefly summarizes the tests that we run, andInternet Appendix IIprovides additional details.

First, we show that MMA exposure is unrelated to several key banks’ characteristics. Although few bank characteristics are correlated with the continuous or dummy MMA exposure measure, the results are unchanged when controlling for such characteristics. In addition, we show that the results are unchanged when controlling for (a) holdings of noneligible securities, to rule out concerns related to possible general equilibrium effects that could affect banks through their holdings of such securities; (b) banks’ exposure to other ECB policies; and (c) the comprehensive assessments conducted in 2013–2014 (ie, stress tests and asset quality reviews).

To deal with the concern of anticipatory bias, we first note that the MMA PSPP exposure is highly persistent: the autocorrelation coefficient is 0.67 and 0.92 in the 6 months before the 2019 and 2015 announcements, respectively. We then redefine a bank as exposed if it held a large amount of MMA sovereign securities in both the preannouncement month and 6 months before, and we rerun our main regression. The results are unchanged.

To exclude the possibility that our results mechanically reflect generic differences in banks’ business models that correlate with their AFS portfolios, we consider a falsification test. We measure a bank’s 2015 exposure based on eligible securities classified as AFS and in the trading book—the criteria used to compute the MMA exposure in 2019. The results are insignificant.

Finally, we perform two additional tests. To deal with seasonality concerns, we expand our sample from 1 to 4 years for each announcement—with 33 million observations for the 2019 tests and 23 million for the 2015 ones—and we control for seasonal effects by including monthly dummies interacted with the PSPP exposure. The main results are again unchanged. We then perform a placebo test, repeating our main analysis 1 year before the first PSPP announcement and showing that holdings of MMA PSPP eligible securities at that time do not affect lending.
article.5. Mechanism:
We have seen robust evidence that the macroprudential accounting regime mediates the impact of QE onto bank lending. This section now examines the channels of causation, focusing on the recapitalization channel, the liquidity channel, and the portfolio rebalancing channel. In a final suite of tests, we use both firm- and bank-level heterogeneity to better understand the loan supply response and the allocation of credit. Our findings show that capital gains on MMA eligible securities are the key drivers of the increase in lending, supporting theregulatory capitalrecapitalization channel.

5.1. Liquidity, portfolio rebalancing, and recapitalization

The first set of tests uses a triple interaction approach to examine whether banks’ ex ante heterogeneity in regulatory capital or liquidity shapes their lending responses to the PSPP. These tests are related to the recapitalization channel and the liquidity channel, respectively, as discussed inSection 2. We use Tier 1 capital as a share of risk-weighted assets to measure banks’ heterogeneity in capitalization, and central bank reserves as a fraction of total assets to measure bank-level heterogeneity in liquidity. These two variables are centered at the 25th percentile of their respective distributions, so that the results can be easily interpreted as the marginal effect relative to banks in the bottom quartile of the capital and liquidity distributions.30We then interact banks’ ex ante capital with their own MMA exposure (ie, the holdings of MMA PSPP eligible securities relative to total assets) and an indicator variable for the month of the PSPP announcement, and we do the same for liquidity. We focus on the announcement month, as the bulk of the supply response occurs at that time (seeSection 4). We also consider the interaction between capital and liquidity, as both theoretical and empirical considerations suggest that banks’ lending decisions might depend not just on capitalization and liquidity positions alone but also on the interplay between them.

The results, shown inTable 6, are consistent with the recapitalization channel. The coefficient for the triple interaction between a bank’s Tier 1 capital ratio, MMA exposure, and announcement month dummy is negative and significant in both 2019 (column 1) and 2015 (column 2), suggesting that the impact of the PSPP on lending among MMA-exposed banks is significantly lower for more capitalized banks. Consider a bank at the median level of MMA exposure, conditional on strictly positive values. The 2019 coefficients suggest that lending increased after the announcement by about 0.83 percentage points if the bank is at the 25th percentile of the Tier 1 ratio distribution, but only 0.57 percentage points if the bank is at the 75th percentile (ie, about 30% less). In 2015, the effects related to banks’ capitalization are larger. For a bank at the 25th percentile, the impulse is 0.6 percentage points and essentially zero at the 75th percentile. That the recapitalization channel is stronger in 2015 is not surprising, given that Italian banks were less capitalized in 2015 relative to 2019 (see, for instance, the IMF Country Report No. 20/81). Of note is that we detect no effects related to banks’ holdings of liquidity—neither directly nor in relation to their capital position.

While the results ofTable 6are consistent with a recapitalization channel, they could also be driven by a portfolio rebalancing channel: less capitalized banks could sell more PSPP-eligible securities to invest in loans, which have a higher expected return. We thus assess the portfolio rebalancing channel in two steps: first, we examine the impact of the PSPP on trading activity, and second, we gauge the importance of this trading activity relative to the recapitalization channel in shaping the post-announcement loan supply among more MMA-exposed banks. Note, however, that only half of this channel might operate. That is, exposed banks could sell eligible appreciated securities to hoard liquidity, reduce their borrowing from the interbank market, or reduce noncore liabilities, leaving the loan supply unchanged.

In the first step, we use the granularity of the supervisory data to measure banks’ trading activity. The unit of observation is the trading of security s by bank b at time t , and a security is defined at the ISIN level—the most disaggregated. 31 For each bank b , we define the trading of security s in month t as the change in the quantity of security s over month t times the average market price in month t , scaled by the bank’s assets in the previous month: T r a d i n g s , b , t = ( Q u a n t i t y s , b , t − Q u a n t i t y s , b , t − 1 ) × P r i c e s , t a v e r a g e A s s e t s b , t − 1 .

This variable is positive if the bank is a net buyer of securitysand negative if the bank is a net seller. The estimating regression usesTradings,b,tas the dependent variable and is otherwise similar to our baseline specification, but we followPeydró, Polo, and Sette (2021)and (a) control for security-by-time fixed effects to absorb the influence of time-varying security-level factors that affect all banks equally and (b) cluster standard errors at the bank and ISIN level.32

To better identify the role of macroprudential regulations, we analyze the trading activity separately for the trading book and available-for-sale (AFS) portfolios. In 2019, trading book and AFS securities were both valued at MMA, but in 2015, only the trading book securities were valued at MMA, as AFS securities were valued at HCA. This implies that for an AFS security with unrealized losses, such losses are not included in 2015’s regulatory capital, but they would be included if a bank were to sell the security. This incentive to shield capital from unrealized AFS losses would then dampen any trading response to the 2015 PSPP announcement in the AFS portfolio. In contrast, since AFS assets were already valued at MMA in 2019, there is no major regulatory friction to trading AFS assets in response to the 2019 PSPP announcement. Thus, we should expect a bigger AFS trading response in 2019 relative to 2015.

Regarding the trading book, there should be little difference in PSPP-related trading activity when comparing 2019 with 2015. Unlike AFS securities, trading book assets were valued using MMA in both 2015 and 2019. Also, any trading book response to the PSPP might be small or insignificant, as trading book assets are rarely used for asset liability management; these assets are ordinarily used to generate trading income. Thus, any rebalancing in response to the PSPP is unlikely to be concentrated in the trading book.

Consistent with the first step in the rebalancing channel,Figure 5suggests that MMA exposure to the PSPP elicited a larger AFS trading response in 2019. In panel A, the dependent variable is the monthly trading of AFS PSPP-eligible securities between March 2019 and February 2020, and we regress it on both the MMA exposure interacted with monthly dummies (coefficients in the top left panel) and the HCA exposure interacted with monthly dummies (coefficients in the top right panel); the results are also reported inTable III.I, inInternet Appendix III. The effects of the HCA exposure are economically and statistically insignificant, but there is a significant impulse in trading activity after the PSPP announcement among banks with larger MMA exposures.

We have seen evidence that banks more exposed to the 2019 PSPP announcement increased their trading of eligible AFS securities. While this first step is consistent with the rebalancing channel, banks might not have used the proceeds of the sales to increase lending. We now evaluate the relative importance of any PSPP-related rebalancing in determining the lending response of banks. To compare the portfolio rebalancing and recapitalization channels, we construct bank-level measures of capital gains on MMA eligible securities as well as bank-level measures of trading revenues from eligible securities in a narrow 1-month window around each PSPP announcement. These two measures capture theregulatory capitalrecapitalization channel (ie, the recapitalization channel that affects regulatory capital) and the portfolio rebalancing channel, respectively.

We measure banks’ capital gains on MMA eligible securities as the product of the lagged quantity and the change in price, relative to total assets: CapitalGains b , t = ∑ s ( Quantity s , b , t − 1 × ( Price s , t − Price s , t − 1 ) ) Assets b , t − 1 .

Likewise, the bank-level measure of trading revenues is computed by adding up the ISIN-level measure Trading s , b , t introduced before: 35 Trading b , t = ∑ s ( Quantity s , b , t − Quantity s , b , t − 1 ) × Price s , t average Assets b , t − 1 .

The variableTradingb,tis constructed using eligible securities in the AFS and trading book portfolio, butInternet Appendix III.4shows that the results are unchanged if we restrict attention to only one portfolio or the other, or if we consider all securities, including those that are not eligible to be purchased under the PSPP. BothCapitalGainsb,tandTradingb,tare computed in the month immediately upon the PSPP’s announcements, and because of the way they are defined, they measure capital gains and trading activity relative to the month just before. These two measures thus capture the cross-sectional variation in banks’ capital gains and revenues from trading activity just around the two PSPP announcements, respectively.Figures 6and7plot the distribution of capital gains and revenues from trading activity in the announcement month and the following few months, respectively, confirming that most of the effect was immediate.

We find no evidence that trading activity over the same narrow window is associated with any increase in lending.

5.2. The allocation of credit

The evidence based on bank-level heterogeneity suggests that the PSPP increased lending mainly via the recapitalization channel and, in particular, by increasing banks’ regulatory capital. This section combines bank- and firm-level heterogeneity to study the allocation of credit in the wake of the two PSPP announcements.38A key objective is to assess what role, if any, the macroprudential accounting regime played in shaping banks’ risk-taking behavior in the corporate loan book.

The “risk-taking channel” predicts heterogeneity in the types of firms that obtain credit after expansionary policies like the PSPP (Bauer, Bernanke, and Milstein 2023;Kashyap and Stein 2023). This channel notes that expansionary policies can increase the risk appetite of financial intermediaries, increasing loan supply to riskier firms. But the risk-taking channel also notes that bank-level heterogeneity can shape the allocation of credit across firm types. For example, monetary stimulus can help undercapitalized banks derisk, so that these banks might lend less to riskier firms after the PSPP (Pegoraro 2024;Peydró, Polo, and Sette 2021). Regulatory considerations might also affect the risk-taking response of banks, as riskier loans might require banks to lock in more capital. Thus, less capitalized banks might prefer to lend to less risky firms to take more advantage of the ability to expand lending. Bank-level heterogeneity can also shape the allocation of credit across firm types.

Building on these arguments,Table 8examines the allocation of credit after the PSPP announcements. We modify the baseline specification ofEquation (1)by interacting measures of firms’ type with a bank’s MMA exposure around the PSPP’s announcement. Because this approach results in a series of triple interaction terms and their lower order counterparts, which greatly multiply the number of parameters to be estimated, we focus again on the credit supply response in the month of the announcement. That is, we interact the firm’s type with the bank’s MMA exposure along with an indicator variable for the month of the PSPP announcement. As shown in the previous sections, much of the lending response was immediate, and little information is lost with this approach.

We begin with the 2019 analysis, the results of which are reported in the top panel ofTable 8. Because firm-level data are available only for a subset of the firms included in the baseline regression, column 1 reproduces the baseline result using this subsample—and the more concise specification. We again find that lending disproportionately increased in the month of the PSPP’s announcement among MMA-exposed banks even within the restricted sample.

Column 2 considers the role of firm risk in the allocation of credit, adding the interaction with an indicator variable that equals one for risky firms. Credit risk is measured on a 0–10 scale, with higher scores denoting a riskier firm, and we set our indicator variable to one for firms with a score greater than or equal to 7, and zero otherwise; about 15% of the firms in our sample are flagged as risky. The interaction term is negative and significant, implying that riskier firms received less bank credit after the PSPP announcement. A bank at the median MMA exposure increased lending by 0.71 percentage points to nonrisky firms but only by 0.12 percentage points to risky firms. Column 3 adds an interaction term with firm size, which is an indicator variable that equals 1 if the firm has revenues that exceed €50 million, and zero otherwise. The result that MMA-exposed banks lent to safer firms in the wake of the 2019 PSPP is unchanged, and column 3 also shows that such banks directed their lending more toward smaller firms.

We have seen evidence that the PSPP increased lending via the recapitalization channel, and column 4 ofTable 8combines bank- and firm-level heterogeneity to examine whether banks’ ex ante regulatory capital also shaped risk-taking incentives. To this end, we use a quadruple interaction term. We interact a bank’s MMA exposure with the PSPP announcement month dummy, the indicator variable that equals 1 if a firm is high risk, and the bank’s Tier 1 capital ratio (ie, the ratio of Tier 1 capital to risk-weighted assets). We include the lower-order interactions as well. The quadruple term is positive and significant, suggesting that the most undercapitalized MMA-exposed banks lent relatively less to riskier firms after the 2019 PSPP announcement—riskier banks derisked the most.

Consider a bank that is at the median MMA exposure and at the 75th percentile of the Tier 1 ratio distribution. For such a bank, the loan supply to nonrisky firms grew 0.37-percentage-points more than the loan supply to risky firms. For an otherwise identical but less capitalized bank—at the 25th percentile of the Tier 1 ratio distribution—the gap is wider at 0.64 percentage points. The result about the higher derisking by less capitalized banks might appear at odd with some of the literature that analyses banks’ response to other unconventional ECB interventions. For instance,Acharya et al. (2019)show that the Outright Monetary Transactions (OMT) program recapitalized banks, similar to our finding, but weakly capitalized banks shifted their supply toward low-quality borrower. We note, however, that between the launch of the OMT in 2012 and the PSPP announcement in 2015, many banks went through a comprehensive assessment (ie, asset quality review and stress tests) that forced them to improve their capital position.39Thus, Italian banks in our sample had better capital positions than a few years earlier, when the OMT was launched.

The remaining columns ofTable 8exploit the firm-level heterogeneity to use bank-by-time fixed effects as an additional robustness check.40The evidence continues to suggest that banks more exposed to the PSPP lent less to riskier firms after the PSPP (column 5), though the quadruple interaction term in column 6 is less precisely estimated.

Finally, the bottom panel ofTable 8replicates the 2019 exercise for the 2015 PSPP announcement. The results are similar to those derived for 2019: the PSPP has a weaker impact on the credit supplied to riskier firms, derisking is relatively bigger among the less capitalized banks, and the results are robust to including bank-by-time fixed effects.
article.6. Extension: Other Monetary Policy Shoc:
This section examines whether our results extend to other monetary policy tools in different circumstances. A possible concern is that our results are based on a specific QE policy—the PSPP—which, despite being the largest ECB asset purchases program, was implemented in response to specific economic circumstances. That is, the concern is that the results may not generalize to other monetary tools, such as forward guidance, that also aim to influence the longer end of the yield curve. This section, however, shows that the pass-through of any monetary policy shock to the long-end of the yield curve—not just QE—is mediated by the macroprudential accounting regime.

To gauge the generalizability of our results, we broaden the estimation window to span the period July 2010—when the macroprudential accounting rules switch to HCA for available-for-sale (AFS) securities—through February 2020—just before the COVID-19 pandemic.41We then examine the effects of monetary policy shocks to the long end of the yield curve—so-called “pathrate shocks”—on bank loan supply. We use shocks from the Euro Area Monetary Policy Event-Study Database (EA-MPD,Altavilla et al. 2019), which are extracted from asset prices in 10-minute windows bracketing the ECB’s regular release of monetary policy information after each of its 10 or so meetings per year. By examining the high-frequency change in bond yields around these announcements, the EA-MPD shocks can help us measure the loan supply responses that are free of anticipatory biases. The EA-MPD also reports shocks to the short end of the yield curve—targetrate shocks—but during our sample period, the policy or target rate was at the zero lower bound, and most target rate shocks were very small (Figure 9, dotted line). In contrast, shocks to the long end of the yield curve were often sizeable, as forward guidance and other monetary policy interventions altered longer-term yields even while the policy rate was at the zero lower bound. We use shocks to 10-year yields on Italian bonds (Figure 9, solid line), as Italian bonds represent the large majority of sovereign assets held by Italian banks.
