Our dependent variable is the monthly loan growth rate at the bank-firm level, as in our baseline specification. We then use a monthly time seriesIT10tof the path rate shocks—shocks to 10-year Italian bond yields in the window around the ECB’s statement and press conference—and interact this series with a bank’s 1-month-lagged sovereign MMA exposure, scaled by the bank’s assets. Note that because we consider path rate shocks on 10-year Italian bonds (ie, on long-term yields), we measure MMA exposure as the value of sovereign bonds on a bank’s balance sheet with a residual maturity of at least 5 years (ie, long-term sovereign bonds) that are subject to MMA. As before, we employ firm-by-month fixed effects to absorb latent demand as well as bank fixed effects, along with the other suite of controls employed in our baseline analysis.42

Consistent with the idea that a decline in long-term rates can increase MMA-based asset valuations, banks’ regulatory capital, and with it, bank lending, column 1 ofTable 9shows that the interaction between the path rate shock seriesIT10tand a bank’s MMA exposure is negative and significant. This result suggests that a negative monetary policy shock to the longer end of the yield curve positively affects bank lending among MMA-exposed banks. In response to a 1-percentage-point negative shock, a bank in the median of the 2010–2017 sovereign MMA exposure—when MMA was restricted to the trading book—increases lending by 0.08%.43However, for a bank at the median of the 2018–2020 exposure—when MMA was extended to available-for-sale (AFS) securities—the increase in lending is 2.65%.

The value of shorter-duration bonds is less sensitive to interest rate movements, and shorter-term interest rates did not move much in our sample period, as noted before. In keeping with these facts, column 2 shows that holdings of sovereign bonds with a maturity less than 5 years do not trigger any sizeable response of loan supply in response to shorter-term path rate shocks—those derived from the 2-year Italian government bond—while the effect of 10-year rate shocks remains unchanged.

Because the PSPP announcements are sizeable shocks to the long end of the yield curve, column 3 drops the 2015 and 2019 PSPP announcement months from the sample period to isolate the effect of other shocks. The point estimate remains significant and suggests that shocks other than QE to the long end of the yield curve can engender a sizeable loan supply response, too.

The remaining columns build on our previous tests to deepen our understanding of the role of MMA in transmitting these more general monetary policy shocks onto bank loan supply decisions. To do this, column 4 ofTable 9creates apost–January 2018dummy variable that equals 1 in the months after regulators switched from HCA to MMA to value AFS sovereign assets (ie, after January 2018), and 0 otherwise. We then interact this dummy variable with the lag ratio of AFS sovereign assets to total bank assets and the monetary policy shock seriesIT10t⁠. We also include all lower-order interaction terms. If MMA helps transmit these shocks onto bank lending, then AFS sovereign assets should affect lending decisions after a shock only when those assets are valued using MMA. That is, the coefficient for the interactionIT10t×AFSshould be negative and significant only when interacted with thepostdummy. Column 4 also interacts the monetary policy shock seriesIT10twith bond holdings that were always valued at MMA throughout the sample period (ie, those in the trading book) and separately with those holdings that were always valued using HCA (ie, those held to maturity), all scaled by bank assets.

Consistent with the previous evidence based on the PSPP, the coefficient forIT10t×AFS×postis negative and significant, but the coefficient forIT10t×AFSis not significant. The coefficient that refers to trading book assets—always valued at MMA—is also negative and significant, and the coefficient for held-to-maturity assets—always valued at HCA—is not significant. This suggests that the relative size of a bank’s MMA assets, possibly through the regulatory capital recapitalization channel, is an important element of the monetary transmission mechanism.

Consistent with the salience of the regulatory capital recapitalization channel, column 5 returns to the baseline case (column 1) and shows that the impact of monetary policy shocks on lending via MMA exposure is significantly weaker for the better-capitalized banks.44For a bank at the median MMA exposure level (conditional on positive MMA holdings), a 1-percentage-point negative shock is associated with a 0.41-percentage-point increase in lending if the bank is also at the 25th percentile of Tier 1 capital to risk weighted assets, but 0.29 percentage points if the bank is at the 75th percentile (ie, almost 30% lower). And similar toSection 5.1, we find no effects of the liquidity position of the bank.

These results (and a few robustness tests inInternet Appendix VIII) suggest that the transmission onto bank lending of other monetary policy shocks that influence long-term bond yields depends on the macroprudential accounting regime and, in particular, on banks’ MMA exposure. We view this evidence as suggestive, as the monthly time series of path rate shocks does not afford the “clean” event-study type identification of the PSPP. Despite this caveat,Table 9suggests that the PSPP results generalize to other settings and policy tools aimed at the longer end of the yield curve.
article.7. Discussion: Macroprudential Accountin:
We now discuss some broader implications of the results derived in the previous sections. First, we propose alternative macroprudential regulations that prevent credit crunches induced by increases in sovereign default spreads without muting the pass-through of monetary policy. Second, we argue that macroprudential accounting rules can affect stabilization tools beyond monetary policy. Specifically, we argue that HCA interacts with the capital requirement regulation.

As a first step, we recast our key result in the context of a very simple framework. Consider a bank with deposits d t − 1 and two types of assets: loans l t − 1 ⁠ , and sovereign securities with face value g purchased at t— 1 at price p t − 1 and with t + 1 maturity. The sovereign securities are valued at price p , which depends on the macroprudential accounting regime: if the bank uses HCA, p = p t − 1 ⁠ ; if instead the bank uses MMA, p = p t ⁠ . The bank is subject to a risk-weighted capital requirement with zero risk weight on sovereign securities and 100% risk weight on loans: capital t ≥ ξ l t ,

where ξ is the minimum level of the capital that banks need to maintain, and capital t = l t − 1 + p × g − d t − 1 .

Our first observation is that the framework generates the results that we have derived—about the role of HCA in the pass through of monetary policy—provided that the capital requirement constraint is binding. In particular, when such a constraint is binding, the growth rate of lending is proportional to the accounting value of the sovereign securities, 45 l t − l t − 1 l t − 1 ∝ p g .

Under HCA (ie,p=pt−1⁠), a change in the market priceptinduced by movement in yields is not transmitted to regulatory capital and, thus, does not affect bank lending. This explains why European regulators have been using HCA to shield banks from changes in sovereign default spreads, and why the PSPP had almost no effect on lending in 2015, when HCA was prevalent.

Under MMA, the price at which the securities g are valued is p t = 1 1 + i t + s t ⁠ , where i t is the risk-free rate set by the monetary authority, and s t is the sovereign default spread. The growth rate of loans is thus l t − l t − 1 l t − 1 ∝ 1 1 + i t + s t g .

A reduction initincreases banks’ capital and allows banks to lend more. This captures, for instance, the PSPP in 2019 or forward guidance shocks after 2018, when MMA was prevalent. However, an increase in sovereign default spreads also pass through onto the balance sheet and reduce lending.

Our second remark is that a hybrid accounting rule eliminates the trade-off between protecting banks from changes in sovereign spreads and allowing monetary policy to pass through. That is, if banks value sovereign securities at the price p t h y b r i d = 1 1 + i t + s t − 1 that fixes default spreads at a historical level, s t − 1 ⁠ , but uses the current risk-free rate i t , the growth rate of lending would be l t − l t − 1 l t − 1 ∝ 1 1 + i t + s t − 1 g .

This approach requires banks and regulators to separate the yield of risky sovereign debt into a risk-free component and a default premium component. In practice, this does not appear particularly complicated.

A different approach that produces results identical to those of the hybrid rule uses MMA to value assets (ie, the market value of capital) together with time-varying capital requirements that depend on market prices and banks’ balance sheets to target a particular loan supply l t ⁠ : capital t M M A ≥ ξ t ∗ l t , where ξ t ∗ is the ratio of the MMA-based value of capital and the hybrid rule-based value of capital: ξ t ∗ = capital t M M A capital t h y b r i d .

The time-varying capital requirement decreases when the sovereign default spread goes up (ie, when the MMA-based value of capital goes down), fully offsetting the impact of such an increase and, thus, preventing credit crunches. That is,ξt∗is a countercyclical time-varying capital requirement that avoids the pitfalls of HCA in relation to the pass-through of monetary policy.

Our last observation is that HCA interacts with capital requirements. This is because our results are rationalized in a setting with a binding capital requirement constraint, and because HCA influences the level of regulatory capital. When the central bank reduces the interest rate, our results have shown that HCA mutes the pass through onto bank lending. What we observe here is that coupling the central bank intervention with an appropriate reduction in capital requirements can undo the effects of HCA, producing the same lending response that would be obtained under MMA.46The flip side of this argument is that the combination of HCA and astaticcapital requirement—frequently used in practice—is equivalent to MMA combined with a tightening of the capital requirement in response to changes in yields. Note that the logic extends to changes in yields driven by any exogenous shock and not just those related to monetary policy. For instance, the logic is the same if we focus on shocks that change sovereign default premiums.

To gauge the magnitude of the link between HCA and capital requirements, we provide a simple quantification building on the results ofSection 6; details about the calculations are provided inInternet Appendix VI. Consider a 1-percentage-point reduction in the yield on 10-year bonds, and a bank with (a) the average holdings of sovereign securities in the period 2010–2017—when HCA was prevalent—and (b) a 10% Tier 1 ratio.47If MMA applies to all the assets of the bank, the bank would increase lending by 5.41%. However, most assets were valued at HCA at that time, and the bank would have increased lending by only 0.40%. This latter figure could be obtained under MMA if the change in yields is coupled with an 8.32% tightening of the capital requirement. This is a large effect, as monetary cycles might result in changes in yields that are three or four times bigger than what we have considered here. In essence, HCA mutes the monetary policy efforts to stimulate the economy—as well as the effects of any reduction in yields—by raising the effective capital requirement that is applied to the market value of banks.
article.8. Conclusion:
This paper has used Italian credit register and granular supervisory data to study the role of macroprudential accounting regulation in affecting the pass-through of monetary policy onto banks’ loan supply to firms. Our main result is that macroprudential accounting regulation that uses historical cost to compute regulatory capital mutes the transmission of monetary policy onto bank lending, and it arises because the use of historical cost limits the recapitalization of banks and, more precisely, the pass-through of monetary policy ontoregulatorycapital. While our focus is on the ECB’s 2015 and 2019 announcements of the PSPP—the ECB’s largest QE program—our results extend to other monetary policy shocks to the long end of the yield curve.

To place these results in context, we have noted that a large literature has generally reported mixed results on the real effects of QE with little clarity on the factors behind the wide range of estimates. A related and mostly theoretical literature has also wrestled with whether monetary and macroprudential policies are complements or substitutes. This latter literature has generally focused on macroprudential tools in the form of capital and liquidity regulations. Separately, the literature on accounting itself has mainly focused on the trade-off between market discipline and the costs of excess volatility and contagion. Thus, ours is the first paper, to our knowledge, to use micro data and a plausible identification strategy to study how macroprudential accounting rules can sharply mediate the real effects of yield curve management policies on firms’ access to credit. Our results help explain why estimates of the effect of QE might vary across studies. More generally, our results show that macroprudential accounting policies can directly interfere with other macro-stabilization tools. Besides their effect on limiting monetary policy, we have highlighted the link between macroprudential accounting policies and capital requirements, and we have suggested policies that can achieve the stabilization benefits of macroprudential accounting rules while allowing the pass-through of central bank interventions.

Beyond its policy implications, this paper opens up several directions for future research. Our results suggest that analyses of the trade-off between historical cost and mark-to-market accounting should also incorporate the impact of these accounting regimes on other major macro-stabilization policies, such as monetary policy and capital requirements. Future research can further examine the interaction between these policies as well as others, such as the post-2010 liquidity policy. Our evidence can also help researchers develop more realistic quantitative models of banking regulation and monetary policy that account for the macroprudential accounting regulations.

2 The active use of accounting rules in banking regulation began in the early 1990s in the wake of the savings and loans crisis. Mark-to-market accounting was intended to prevent banks from hiding bad assets and “gains trading” their good assets, and historical cost accounting was intended to reduce the volatility of equity and earnings ( Ellul et al. 2015 ; Greenspan 1990 ; Shaffer 1992 ).

3 Accounting-based macroprudential policies are employed in other jurisdictions too. In the United States, HCA is used by most banks, MMA is prevalent only for larger “advanced approaches” banks ( Fuster and Vickery 2018 ; Kim, Kim, and Ryan 2019 ), and the prevalence of HCA increased when interest rates rose in both 1994–1995 and 2022. In particular, in 2022, regulators allowed six large banks to move $500 billion in assets from the MMA to HCA classification, as reported by the Wall Street Journal ( Weil 2023 ). In Australia, MMA is prevalent ( Lonsdale 2023 ).

4 In our setting, the risk of deflation and weak credit supply in the aftermath of the European sovereign crisis spurred the 2015 PSPP announcement, while improved economic conditions fostered the 2018 change in macroprudential policy toward MMA.

5 For example, different macroprudential accounting rules apply for an asset recorded in the trading book on a bank’s balance sheet relative to the same asset recorded as held to maturity. As a result, analyses based on a broad aggregation of a bank’s asset holdings suffer from considerable measurement error and cannot identify the relevant transmission channels.

6 Any demand explanation would thus have to posit that loan demand increased after the PSPP announcement, in both 2015 and 2019. This possibility is highly unlikely and is inconsistent with the reduction in interest rates that we document, as an increase in demand should produce an increase in interest rates.

7 Some have argued that MMA propagated shocks through the financial system in 2008–2009; see Allen and Carletti (2008) and Plantin, Sapra, and Shin (2008) for models that discuss the potential costs of MMA in bank capital regulation. Ellul et al. (2015) provide evidence on some of the potential drawbacks of HCA in the context of the U.S. insurance industry.

8 An important literature has examined the impact of monetary policy when rates are negative. For example, Altavilla et al. (2022) find that negative rates can lead to more firm investment. Bottero et al. (2022) show that negative rates increase credit supply through a portfolio rebalancing channel. Bittner et al. (2023) study the interaction between QE and negative rates, noting that negative rates might impair the transmission of QE.

9 Other papers study macroprudential policies alone using credit register data; notably, Jiménez et al. (2017) focus on countercyclical capital buffers. Some macroprudential policies also encompass household leverage (see, e.g., DeFusco, Johnson, and Mondragon 2020 ).

10 A sizeable literature tackles the transmission of QE through the mortgage and housing channel. See, for example, Di Maggio et al. (2017) , Beraja et al. (2019) , Di Maggio, Kermani, and Palmer (2020) , and Ramcharan (2020) .

11 Other channels, such as the signaling channel, are not as easily testable. The signaling channel posits that because QE programs are difficult to reverse, programs like the PSPP signal a central bank’s commitment to prolonged low short-term rates. However, the cross-sectional variation in these beliefs is difficult to observe, making it difficult to empirically quantify the importance of channels like signaling.

12 IFRS 9, which has been applied since 2018, says that an entity can classify its securities in three ways: amortized cost , fair value through other comprehensive income , and fair value through profit or loss . We map these categories onto those of the IAS 39 framework, that is, held to maturity , available for sale , and trading book , respectively.

13 Under the IAS 39 rules in place until 2018, the reclassification of a held-to-maturity security is generally not allowed, and if it takes place, it can trigger the reevaluation of all banks’ holdings based on market prices. This trigger is part of the so-called “tainting rule.” Under IFRS 9, in place since 2018, reclassifications are only possible when an entity changes its business model for managing financial assets, such as in the event of mergers and acquisitions. In the case of available for sale versus trading book, if a bank were to classify securities as available for sale but then trade them frequently—as if they were in the trading book—regulators can flag this behavior and take corrective actions.

14 Formally, AFS securities were always evaluated using MMA, but between 2010 and 2017, regulators allowed banks to omit unrealized gains and losses from the computation of regulatory capital, which is equivalent to using HCA for the purpose of computing regulatory capital. This is analogous to the so-called “AOCI filter” in the United States. See CEBS (2004) , Regulation EU no. 575/2013 (page 508), Article 467, and Bank of Italy (2010) .

15 As the COVID-19 crisis hit in 2020—after the end of our sample period—European regulators allowed banks to again use HCA for available-for-sale securities; see Bank of Italy (2020) .

16 Because the exact criteria for international institutions were not fully clarified at the time, we consider as eligible the debt of supranational institutions with main headquarters in a euro area country. See the technical annex from the European Central Bank’s (2015) press release. In addition, the securities had to fulfil the collateral eligibility criteria for the ECB and must have had a credit rating of at least CQS3 (i.e., Credit Quality Step 3). This corresponds to at least BBB- for S&P’s and Fitch and Baa3 for Moody’s.

17 In 2019, purchases were estimated at €20 billion per month, and debt issued by euro area local governments was eligible to be purchased under the program, too. See Decision (EU) 2015/2464 of the European Central Bank.
